decks.name: English Riddles — Fluent
decks.description: 15 énigmes : des mots anglais subtils et idiomatiques, pour un niveau avancé.

clues: I am a long noun that many people call their favourite English word.
I describe finding something you were not looking for.
Penicillin was discovered thanks to me.
I mean a happy accident, a fortunate discovery made by chance.
I have five syllables and I start with "seren-".
I am a verb you probably practise more than you admit.
Students know me very well the night before an exam.
"I'll do it tomorrow" is my motto.
Deadline approaching and you are still scrolling? You are doing me.
I mean to keep postponing what you should do now.
I am a verb whose strange origin involves the edge of a roof.
Spies are professionals at me.
You might do me while standing behind a door.
It is rude to do me during private conversations.
I mean to secretly listen to what other people are saying.
I am a bittersweet feeling.
Old songs and photo albums often trigger me.
I come from Greek words meaning "return" and "pain".
I make you miss "the good old days".
I am the longing for the past.
I am both a physical place and an abstract limit.
Newlyweds are traditionally carried over me.
Scientists measure your "pain" version of me.
Cross me and you are inside the house.
I am the strip at the bottom of a doorway, or the point where something begins.
I am a negative thing that people "hold".
I can last for years after a tiny argument.
Forgiveness is my worst enemy.
If you still resent your friend for something from 2010, you hold me.
I am a persistent feeling of resentment against someone.
People say I am always "20/20".
I only work after things have happened.
With me, everyone is a genius.
I am the opposite of foresight.
I mean understanding a situation clearly, but only once it is over.
Lawyers love to find me.
I am a gap that nobody intended to leave.
Clever people slip through me to escape the rules.
Tax specialists hunt for me in the law.
I am a small flaw in a rule that lets you avoid it legally.
I am a German loanword commonly used in English.
Travel blogs and Instagram captions overuse me.
I am an itch you cannot scratch at home.
I make you buy plane tickets on a whim.
I am a strong desire to travel and explore the world.
I am also the name of a Christian holiday in January.
Scientists and artists hope for me.
I often strike in the shower.
Archimedes shouted "Eureka!" when he had me.
I am a sudden moment of insight or revelation.
Psychologists and coaches talk about me constantly.
Bamboo has me; glass does not.
Life's setbacks put me to the test.
I am the art of bouncing back.
I am the capacity to recover quickly from difficulties.
I am an adjective, and not a flattering one.
Washing dishes and commuting are good examples of me.
My Latin root means "world".
I describe the dull routine of everyday life.
I am a synonym of "ordinary" and "unexciting".
I am a verb, and my direction is downward.
Savings, crowds and hopes can all do me.
I happen gradually, never suddenly.
I am the opposite of "grow", little by little.
I mean to shrink slowly until almost nothing is left.
Everyone has at least one of me.
I make sitcom characters memorable.
I am small, odd and usually harmless.
Collecting rubber ducks might be yours.
I am a peculiar habit or an unusual trait of personality.
I am both a feeling and a way of bending your back.
Detectives follow me even without evidence.
I live in your gut, not in your head.
"I have a ... that something is wrong."
I am an intuition, a guess based on feeling rather than facts.
